disease.name: Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI)
disease.note: Liver injury caused by prescription medications, OTC drugs, or herbal supplements. Accounts for >50% of acute liver failure in developed countries. Acetaminophen (paracetamol) overdose is the single most common cause. Idiosyncratic DILI is unpredictable and can occur at therapeutic doses. Early recognition and drug cessation are critical.

immediateDangerSigns.note: Hy's Law: high risk of acute liver failure

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Acute liver failure (encephalopathy, INR >1.5) -> EMERGENCY ICU; Hy's Law positive -> URGENT hepatology; Mild transaminitis -> DRUG STOP + OBSERVE

historyFeatures.note: Hepatic encephalopathy if acute liver failure
Key history: any new drug, OTC, supplement, or herbal in past 6 months
Intentional overdose or unintentional exceeding max daily dose
Common culprits: kava, green tea extract, ma huang, chaparral
Amoxicillin-clavulanate is most common antibiotic cause
INH is common cause; peaks at 2-3 months

subtypes.name: Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) Hepatotoxicity
subtypes.management: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) IV protocol; NAC most effective within 8h of ingestion but beneficial up to 24h+

subtypes.name: Idiosyncratic DILI
subtypes.management: Immediate drug cessation; supportive care; corticosteroids considered for DRESS or immuno-allergic DILI

laboratory.name: ALT and AST
laboratory.indication: key for pattern recognition: hepatocellular (ALT >> ALP), cholestatic (ALP >> ALT), or mixed pattern

laboratory.name: ALP and GGT
laboratory.indication: cholestatic markers; R value = (ALT/ULN) / (ALP/ULN); R>5 hepatocellular, R<2 cholestatic, 2-5 mixed

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: jaundice severity; Hy's Law: bilirubin >2x ULN + ALT >3x ULN predicts severe DILI

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: synthetic function; prolonged INR signals severe injury

laboratory.name: Acetaminophen/paracetamol level
laboratory.indication: if overdose suspected; Rumack-Matthew nomogram for timing of NAC

laboratory.name: Viral hepatitis serology (HAV, HBV, HCV, HEV)
Serum ceruloplasmin and copper studies
laboratory.indication: rule out Wilson disease especially in young patients

laboratory.name: Abdominal ultrasound
laboratory.indication: rule out biliary obstruction; assess liver parenchyma

laboratory.name: Liver biopsy
laboratory.indication: indicated when: persistent elevation despite drug stop, diagnostic uncertainty, suspected AIH or Wilson overlap, or for clinical trials

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.indication: rule out biliary obstruction; assess hepatic vasculature; steatosis or fibrosis signs

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Drug-Induced Liver Injury; ACG Clinical Guideline: DILI

pathways.criteria: Elevated LFTs with temporal relation to drug
pathways.treatment: IMMEDIATE cessation of suspected offending drug/toxin. Assess causality using RUCAM (Roussel Uclaf Causality Assessment Method) or Maria & Victorino scale. Monitor LFTs, INR, bilirubin twice weekly until improving. Admit if INR >1.5, bilirubin >5, or encephalopathy. Avoid hepatotoxins. The majority of DILI resolves spontaneously within weeks to months after drug stop.

pathways.criteria: Acetaminophen ingestion >7.5g (adult) or >150mg/kg (child) within 24h; or therapeutic doses in at-risk patients with high NAC levels
pathways.treatment: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) IV protocol: 150mg/kg in 200mL D5W over 1h, then 50mg/kg in 500mL D5W over 4h, then 100mg/kg in 1000mL D5W over 16h. Start within 8h of ingestion for best outcome; benefit extends to 24h+ even if late. NAC also beneficial in non-acetaminophen DILI with acute liver failure. Activated charcoal 1g/kg PO if within 4h of ingestion. Monitor liver enzymes, INR, glucose, renal function. Transplant evaluation if progressive despite NAC.

pathways.criteria: Bilirubin >2x ULN + ALT >3x ULN (Hy's Law), or INR >1.5, or signs of liver failure
pathways.treatment: Immediate hospital admission. Drug cessation. NAC IV (same protocol as acetaminophen — may benefit even non-acetaminophen DILI). Monitor closely: daily LFTs, INR, creatinine, glucose, lactate. Evaluate for acute liver failure. Consider transplant evaluation if progressive. If features of DRESS syndrome or immuno-allergic DILI (rash, eosinophilia, fever): consider corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg/day with slow taper). Liver biopsy if uncertain diagnosis.

pathways.criteria: INR >1.5 + any grade hepatic encephalopathy within 26 weeks of jaundice onset
pathways.treatment: ICU admission. NAC IV. Hepatology + transplant team consultation. King's College Criteria for transplant: acetaminophen: pH <7.3 (arterial) OR INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4mg/dL + encephalopathy grade 3-4. Non-acetaminophen: INR >6.5 OR any 3 of: age <10 or >40, jaundice >7 days before encephalopathy, INR >3.5, bilirubin >17.4mg/dL, unfavorable cause (idiosyncratic DILI). Supportive care: intracranial pressure monitoring if encephalopathy, vasopressors, renal replacement therapy, avoid hepatotoxins.
